misconceptionSummary: Students apply positive-number comparison rules directly to negatives, writing −7 > −3 because 7 > 3.

steps.narration: Place both numbers on the number line. Negative seven is seven steps left of zero; negative three is three steps left. On the line, negative seven sits to the left of negative three.

lines.text: Left of another number → smaller value

steps.narration: The number further left is always smaller. Negative seven is to the left of negative three, so negative seven is less than negative three.
The open mouth of the symbol always faces the larger number. Both statements say the same thing — negative seven is the smaller, negative three is the larger.

visuals.label: Further left = smaller — the number line settles it

steps.narration: Negative seven is less than negative three. When in doubt, place both on a number line — whichever sits to the left is the smaller value.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing −7 > −3 because 7 > 3. For negatives, a larger digit means further LEFT — which means a SMALLER value.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Don't compare the digits alone. Place both numbers on a number line — the one on the left is always the smaller, regardless of the digits.
